items.title: FF TEST PHASE 48 — Content Update UX
items.markdownBody:
# TEST PHASE 48 — Content Update UX

This article verifies that release builds check the manifest on every app launch and show updated content without a 24-hour wait.

If you see this article after a GitHub push and app reopen, Phase 48 sync UX is working.

items.title: TEST PHASE 47 — Remote Content Verification
items.markdownBody:
# TEST PHASE 47 — Remote Content Verification

This article verifies that content published to **GKulesza/medbiophys-content** is received by release builds without a new app build.

If you see this article on a real iPhone (English locale) after a GitHub push, remote content sync is working.

items.title: Spring laboratory hours update
items.markdownBody:
# Spring laboratory hours update

The Medical Biophysics laboratory extends opening hours during the spring examination period.

![Practical laboratory](images/news/workshop_lab.png)

## Key changes

- Monday–Thursday: **08:00–18:00**
- Friday: **08:00–15:00**
- Consultations by appointment only

> Please verify your group schedule in the calendar before each session.

Contact the laboratory coordinator if you need a safety refresher.

items.title: Biophysics seminar series opens for spring
items.markdownBody:
# Biophysics seminar series

The department launches its spring seminar series on medical imaging and laboratory diagnostics.

![MRI scanner room](images/news/mri_scan.png)

## First sessions

1. **MRI safety fundamentals** — 18 February
2. **Ultrasound in clinical practice** — 4 March
3. **Optical methods in tissue analysis** — 18 March

Seminars are open to students from all years. [Medical University of Białystok](https://www.umb.edu.pl/) students receive attendance credit toward practical requirements.

items.title: Extended access to radiology reference collection
items.markdownBody:
# Extended library access

The departmental reading room now provides extended evening access to radiology and medical physics references.

![Study space](images/news/library_study.png)

## What's new

- Core journals on MRI, CT, and PET imaging
- Historical archives on **Marie Curie** and early radiology
- Quiet study desks with power outlets

Ask the librarian for the password to digital volumes.

items.title: Department highlights advances in MRI technology
items.markdownBody:
# Advances in MRI technology

Faculty members presented recent work on **7 T research MRI** and quantitative mapping sequences used in neuroimaging.

![MRI technology](images/news/mri_scan.png)

## Highlights

- Faster acquisition with parallel imaging
- Improved fat suppression for musculoskeletal studies
- Open-phased array coils for pediatric anesthesia cases

> MRI remains the gold standard for soft-tissue contrast without ionizing radiation.

items.title: New PET tracer protocols for oncology teaching
items.markdownBody:
# PET imaging update

Clinical partners shared updated **FDG-PET** protocols for teaching cases in oncology and neurology.

![PET scanner](images/news/pet_scanner.png)

## Learning objectives

1. Understand tracer uptake mechanisms
2. Compare SUV normalization methods
3. Interpret fused PET/CT datasets

Students will review anonymized cases during the April workshop.

items.title: Study visit planned to regional proton therapy centre
items.markdownBody:
# Proton therapy study visit

Fourth-year students will visit a regional **proton therapy** facility to observe treatment planning and dose delivery.

![Proton therapy facility](images/news/proton_therapy.png)

## Topics covered

- Bragg peak and spread-out Bragg peak
- Range uncertainty in heterogeneous tissue
- QA measurements with ionization chambers

Registration opens through the Scientific Club board.

items.title: Ultrasound physics module adds hands-on Doppler labs
items.markdownBody:
# Ultrasound physics module

A revised **ultrasound physics** module introduces Doppler flow phantoms and spectral analysis exercises.

![Ultrasound probe](images/news/ultrasound_probe.png)

## Practical skills

- Adjust depth, gain, and focus fields
- Compare continuous-wave vs pulsed Doppler
- Relate beam frequency to axial resolution

Bring your laboratory notebook to the first session.

items.title: Guest lecture on medical AI in radiology reporting
items.markdownBody:
# Medical AI in radiology

A guest lecture explores how **machine learning** assists detection of pulmonary nodules and fracture lines on radiographs.

![Medical AI visualization](images/news/medical_ai.png)

## Discussion points

- Sensitivity vs specificity trade-offs
- Human-in-the-loop reporting workflows
- Regulatory expectations for clinical AI tools

The session includes a live demo of a research segmentation model.

items.title: Hounsfield lecture series celebrates CT imaging pioneers
items.markdownBody:
# Hounsfield lecture series

Named after **Sir Godfrey Hounsfield**, the series revisits the physics of computed tomography and modern spectral CT.

![CT scanner](images/news/hounsfield_ct.png)

## Schedule

1. From filtered back-projection to iterative reconstruction
2. Beam hardening and **Hounsfield units** in practice
3. Dual-energy CT in cardiovascular imaging

items.title: CERN medical applications featured in spring colloquium
items.markdownBody:
# CERN medical applications

The spring colloquium links **CERN accelerator technology** to medical imaging and hadron therapy research.

![Research collider concept](images/news/cern_medical.png)

## Key links

- Particle detectors adapted for PET timing
- Superconducting magnets shared with MRI engineering
- Monte Carlo tools used in radiotherapy planning

Read the [CERN medical applications overview](https://home.cern/science/physics/medical-applications) before attending.

items.title: Scientific Club: iMEDIC 2026 call for abstracts
items.markdownBody:
# Scientific Club: iMEDIC 2026

The Medical Biophysics Scientific Club invites students to submit abstracts for **iMEDIC 2026**.

![Conference hall](images/news/conference_2026.png)

## Important dates

| Milestone | Date |
| --- | --- |
| Abstract deadline | 30 March 2026 |
| Notification | 15 April 2026 |
| Conference | 8–10 May 2026 |

items.title: Student project grants for dosimetry research
items.markdownBody:
# Student project grants

The Scientific Club announces micro-grants for **dosimetry** and radiation protection student projects.

![Student research team](images/news/student_research.png)

## Eligible topics

- Ion chamber calibration studies
- Monte Carlo modeling of treatment fields
- Survey meter intercomparison exercises

items.title: Weekend MRI safety workshop for new members
items.markdownBody:
# MRI safety workshop

New Scientific Club members can join a **MRI safety** workshop covering zone definitions and emergency quench procedures.

![Workshop laboratory](images/news/workshop_lab.png)

## Agenda

1. Static magnetic field hazards
2. RF heating limits and SAR concepts
3. Practical walk-through of Zone II/III boundaries

items.title: Club member receives award for biophysics publication
items.markdownBody:
# Publication award

A Scientific Club member received a departmental award for a **peer-reviewed paper** on optical coherence elastography.

![Journal publication](images/news/publication_journal.png)

## Publication summary

- Measured shear-wave contrast in tissue-mimicking phantoms
- Compared time-domain vs spectral-domain acquisition
- Presented findings at the national medical physics meeting

items.title: National biophysics competition — register your team
items.markdownBody:
# Biophysics competition

Teams of up to four students may register for the **national biophysics competition** hosted by partner universities.

![Competition poster](images/news/competition_poster.png)

## Rounds

1. Online quiz on radiation physics fundamentals
2. Laboratory challenge with uncertainty analysis
3. Oral presentation of a clinical case study

items.title: Fun fact: why MRI magnets are so strong
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: MRI magnets

Clinical MRI scanners use superconducting magnets typically rated at **1.5 T or 3 T**.

![MRI magnet schematic](images/news/magnet_diagram.png)

That is roughly **30,000 times stronger** than Earth's magnetic field. Strict safety zones exist around every scanner room.

items.title: Fun fact: Marie Curie's mobile radiography units
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: Marie Curie

**Marie Curie** developed mobile radiography units during World War I nicknamed *petites Curies*.

![Historical laboratory](images/news/marie_curie_lab.png)

These vehicles brought X-ray diagnostics closer to field hospitals and saved countless surgical decisions.

items.title: Fun fact: what Hounsfield units really measure
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: Hounsfield units

**Hounsfield units (HU)** scale CT voxel values relative to water (0 HU) and air (−1000 HU).

![CT image](images/news/hounsfield_ct.png)

Bone typically exceeds **+400 HU**, while soft tissues cluster near 0–80 HU depending on composition.

items.title: Fun fact: cosmic radiation at flight altitude
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: cosmic radiation

Passengers on long-haul flights receive measurable **cosmic radiation** doses because Earth's atmosphere provides less shielding at altitude.

![Cosmic radiation concept](images/news/cosmic_radiation.png)

Aircrew accumulate annual doses comparable to some medical workers.

items.title: Fun fact: why PET tracers decay so quickly
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: PET tracers

Most clinical PET tracers use **positron-emitting isotopes** with short half-lives, such as fluorine-18 (~110 minutes).

![PET tracer vial](images/news/pet_tracer.png)

Short half-lives limit patient dose but require on-site or nearby cyclotron production.

items.title: Fun fact: the Bragg peak in proton therapy
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: proton range

Protons deposit most of their energy at a sharp **Bragg peak**, allowing dose to stop near the tumor with less exit dose.

![Proton depth-dose curve](images/news/proton_bragg.png)

This physical advantage motivates proton centers for pediatric and ocular treatments.

items.title: Fun fact: Nobel prizes linked to medical imaging
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: Nobel imaging discoveries

Medical imaging owes several **Nobel Prize** breakthroughs, from X-rays (Röntgen, 1901) to MRI (Lauterbur and Mansfield, 2003).

![Nobel imaging collage](images/news/nobel_imaging.png)

Each discovery began as a physics experiment before becoming clinical routine.

items.title: Fun fact: ultrasound frequencies used in clinics
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: ultrasound frequency

Diagnostic ultrasound typically uses **2–18 MHz** probes. Higher frequency improves resolution but reduces penetration depth.

![Doppler ultrasound](images/news/doppler_ultrasound.png)

This trade-off is why abdominal and vascular exams use different probes.

items.title: Fun fact: digital radiography replaced film over two decades
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: digital radiography

**Digital radiography** detectors convert X-rays directly or indirectly into electronic signals, eliminating chemical film processing.

![Digital X-ray detector](images/news/digital_xray.png)

Hospitals gained faster turnaround and lower archive storage costs.

items.title: Fun fact: tissue attenuation differs for every modality
items.markdownBody:
# Fun fact: tissue attenuation

The same tissue attenuates **X-rays**, **ultrasound**, and **MRI signal** through different physical mechanisms.

![Tissue contrast illustration](images/news/tissue_attenuation.png)

Understanding attenuation explains why multimodal imaging is complementary.
